title: How to Remove Scratches From Glasses: Honest Advice
description: Can you really remove scratches from glasses? We cover what works, what doesn't, DIY myths to avoid, and when replacement lenses are the only real fix.
tldr: The honest answer: you can't truly remove scratches from modern coated lenses. DIY remedies (toothpaste, baking soda, car wax) strip the lens coatings and make things worse. Minor surface scratches can sometimes be minimized on uncoated glass lenses, but most prescription glasses today have coated plastic lenses. The real fix is new lenses or scratch-resistant coatings on your next pair.

definition.text: Physical damage to the surface of an eyeglass lens caused by abrasion — contact with hard particles, rough surfaces, or improper cleaning. Scratches scatter light passing through the lens, reducing optical clarity, creating glare, and potentially affecting the integrity of applied coatings.

sections.h2: Why Most Scratch Removal Tips Don't Work
sections.content:
The internet is full of DIY scratch removal tricks: toothpaste, baking soda, baby powder, vehicle rubbing compound, even banana. These all share the same basic idea — use a mild abrasive to buff out the scratch. And they all share the same fundamental problem.

Modern prescription lenses aren't plain glass or plastic. They have multiple applied coatings: anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, UV protection, possibly blue light filtering. These coatings are incredibly thin layers bonded to the lens surface.

When you rub toothpaste or baking soda on a coated lens, you don't fill the scratch. You strip the surrounding coating. The result: the scratch might look slightly less visible, but now you have a larger area of damaged, hazy coating that scatters light worse than the original scratch.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Toothpaste contains fine abrasive particles designed to polish tooth enamel. On an uncoated glass lens (the kind your grandparents wore), toothpaste can sometimes reduce the appearance of very shallow scratches by polishing the surrounding surface to match.

On any modern coated lens — which is virtually every prescription lens sold today — toothpaste is destructive. It strips the AR coating, leaving cloudy patches that are worse than the scratch you started with. The before-and-after photos in online articles usually show uncoated glass lenses or are misleading about the final result.

The same applies to baking soda paste, car polish, brass polish, and furniture wax. These are all abrasives of varying aggressiveness, and they all damage lens coatings.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: When Minor Scratches Can Be Addressed
sections.content:
Uncoated glass lenses: If you have older uncoated glass lenses (increasingly rare), a professional optical lab can sometimes polish out minor surface scratches. This removes a thin layer of glass to smooth the scratch. It only works on shallow scratches and may slightly alter the prescription.

Coating-only damage: If the scratch is only in the coating and not the lens material underneath, some optical labs can strip and reapply the coating. This is possible but not widely offered, and it's often more expensive than simply ordering new lenses.

Plastic lenses (uncoated): Non-prescription sunglasses and safety glasses without coatings can sometimes benefit from plastic polishing compounds. The improvement is usually partial — reducing visibility of the scratch rather than eliminating it.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

For prescription glasses with scratched coatings, replacement lenses are the practical fix. You keep your frames (assuming they're in good shape) and order new lenses with your current prescription.

Replacement lens costs in Canada:

  • Basic single vision: $100–$200 CAD
  • Single vision with premium coatings: $150–$300 CAD
  • Progressive lenses: $300–$600 CAD

Your insurance typically covers replacement lenses on the same schedule as full glasses (every 12–24 months). If you're within your benefit period, new lenses may cost little or nothing out of pocket.

Charm Optical can fit new lenses into your existing frames, and many single vision orders are ready the same day. Start with an eye exam to make sure your prescription is still current — scratched lenses are often a sign your glasses are due for an update anyway.

sections.h2: Preventing Scratches in the First Place
sections.content:
Scratch prevention is far more effective than scratch treatment:

  • Always store in a hard case: The number one cause of scratched glasses is tossing them loose in a bag, pocket, or on a nightstand
  • Rinse before wiping: Dry-wiping grinds dust particles across the lens surface. Rinse under water first, every time
  • Use only microfiber cloths: Paper towels, tissues, and shirt fabric are all abrasive at the microscopic level
  • Place glasses lens-up: If you set them down without a case, lenses face the ceiling, not the table
  • Choose scratch-resistant coating: Modern hard coatings significantly improve scratch resistance (though nothing is scratch-proof)
  • Keep them away from heat: Hot car dashboards and saunas degrade coatings, making them more vulnerable to scratching

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. faqs.q: Does toothpaste really remove scratches from glasses?
faqs.a: No. Toothpaste strips the lens coatings on modern glasses, creating larger cloudy areas that scatter more light than the original scratch. It might work on uncoated glass lenses (very rare today), but it damages the anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings on standard prescription lenses.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Opticians can't repair scratches on coated lenses. They can order replacement lenses for your existing frames, which is the practical solution. Some labs can strip and recoat lenses, but this is usually more expensive than new lenses.

faqs.q: How long do scratch-resistant coatings last?
faqs.a: Quality scratch-resistant coatings last 1–3 years with proper care. They don't prevent all scratches — they resist them. Eventually, accumulated micro-scratches reduce clarity. This timeline aligns with typical prescription update schedules, making natural lens replacement practical.

faqs.q: Are glass lenses more scratch-resistant than plastic?
faqs.a: Yes. Glass is significantly harder than plastic and more resistant to scratching. However, glass is heavier, thicker for strong prescriptions, and shatters rather than flexing on impact. For these reasons, nearly all modern prescription lenses are plastic (CR-39 or polycarbonate) with applied scratch-resistant coatings.

faqs.q: How often should I get my glasses adjusted?
faqs.a: Get a professional adjustment every 3-6 months, or whenever your glasses feel loose, crooked, or uncomfortable. Charm Optical offers free adjustments — just walk in. Regular adjustments extend the life of your frames and keep your vision clear.
